coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 with visible-light-induced high hydrogen evolution catalytic ability
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Shunyan
Li, Chuanxi
Wang, Liping
Liu, Naiyun
Qiao, Shi
Liu, Beibei
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description:
We developed a facile hydrothermal method to fabricate the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/MoS2 composite as hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) catalyst. After irradiation with visible light for 30 min, the CQDs/MoS2 composite (as CQDs/MoS2-L30) performed improved HER activity, featured by a small overpotential of ∼0.125 V at 10 mA cm−2, a high current density, a small Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade and a long term stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. This kind of activity improvement towards HER may be due to the high charge transfer efficiency, the decrease of S4+ and the increase of
S 2
2 −
and S2− after the light treatment.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology , the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) ( 2012CB825803 , 2013CB932702 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51422207 , 51132006 , 51572179 , 21501126 , 21471106 ), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20123201110018 ), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology ( ZXG2012028 ), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the CQDs/MoS2 composite. (a) Low and (b) high magnification TEM images. (c) High resolution TEM image, and the white dotted circles showed the region of CQDs. (d) STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping images of the CQDs/MoS2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online)
(a) XRD patterns of CQDs (black trace), MoS2 (red trace) and the CQDs/MoS2 (blue trace). (b) Raman spectra of CQDs and the CQDs/MoS2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online)

(a) Polarization curves of a bare GCE, the CQDs, MoS2, a physical mixture of MoS2 and CQDs, the CQDs/MoS2 and Pt catalysts with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) The overpotential for driving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and the current density at −0.3 V vs RHE of the catalysts with various initial weight ratios of CQDs/Mo. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online)

Electrochemical characterization (a) Polarization curves in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 on a glassy carbon electrode modified with the CQDs/MoS2 catalysts irradiated for different time. (b) Polarization curves of CQDs/MoS2, CQDs/MoS2-L30 and Pt catalysts with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in 0.5 M H2SO4. (c) Nyquist plots of the CQDs/MoS2 catalysts irradiated for different time. (d) Nyquist plots of the MoS2, CQDs/MoS2, and CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalysts. (e) The Tafel plots of the MoS2, CQDs/MoS2, CQDs/MoS2-L30 and Pt catalysts. (f) Stability tests for the CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst by CV scanning for 1000 cycles. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online)

(a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2-L30. The high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) Mo 3d and (d) S 2p for CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalysts. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online)

Current density-potential (J–P) characteristics with different cycles under visible light on and off conditions of (a) CQDs/MoS2 and (b) CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalysts. The (1) and (1′) showed the polarization curves of different cycles under visible light on (the dotted line) and off (the solid line) conditions, and the (2) and (2′) revealed the overpotentials required to drive 10 mA cm−2 of the various cycles.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 with visible-light-induced high hydrogen evolution catalytic ability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We developed a facile hydrothermal method to fabricate the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/MoS2 composite as hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) catalyst. After irradiation with visible light for 30 min, the CQDs/MoS2 composite (as CQDs/MoS2-L30) performed improved HER activity, featured by a small overpotential of ∼0.125 V at 10 mA cm−2, a high current density, a small Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade and a long term stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. This kind of activity improvement towards HER may be due to the high charge transfer efficiency, the decrease of S4+ and the increase of
S 2
2 −
and S2− after the light treatment.

ce:section.ce:para:
The platinum-based materials as a kind of predominant electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) still suffer from their scarcity and high cost, which are not able to meet the demands for large-scale commercialization [1,2]. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) possesses a graphite-like structure composed of sandwiched S–Mo–S layers, which has got an enormous amount of attention owing to its cost-effective and splendid electrocatalytic activity [3–7]. Previous studies suggested that the HER activity mainly derived from the catalytic active sites existed in the edges of the two-dimensional MoS2 crystalline layers [8–10]. Also, the disulfides
S 2
2 −
and apical S2− were regarded as the active edge sites for HER, which could promote the adsorption of hydrogen atoms [3]. For the design of HER catalyst based on MoS2 with improved electrocatalytic activity, it is crucial to stabilize the structure and expose more active sites in the edges of the MoS2 layers, to obtain good electrical conductivity, and also the chosen conjunct materials should not affect the pristine nature of MoS2 [11,12]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel kind of carbon nanomaterials with small sizes (<10 nm), have received much attention due to their strong fluorescence, rapid electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [13–17]. Our previous work has demonstrated that CQDs could realize the dynamic modulation of electron/energy transfer process at the CQDs-TiO2 interface by a bias-mediated method [18]. We also designed the CQDs/m-BiVO4 complex photocatalyst and confirmed that CQDs had the ability to enhance the photocatalytic activity, but had no negative influence on the m-BiVO4's active facets [19]. Moreover, on account of abundant functional groups on the surface, CQDs as a kind of important component could be readily combined with other compounds and further improved their catalytic properties [20–23]. Notably, the large π-conjugated system of CQDs could make the electron transfer to the MoS2, reducing the number of S4+ and increasing the amount of
S 2
2 −
and S2−.

Based on the above discussion, here, we proposed a catalyst design of combining CQDs with MoS2 as an excellent electrocatalyst for HER. In this study, we fabricated the CQDs/MoS2 composite by a facile hydrothermal method and studied the electrochemical activity towards HER. After irradiated with visible light for 30 min, the composite of CQDs/MoS2-L30 exhibited more efficient electrocatalytic activity for HER with a small overpotential (∼0.125 V at 10 mA cm−2), a high current density, a small Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade and good stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. Further investigation indicated that the improved HER activity of the CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst should be ascribed to the high charge transfer efficiency, the reducing of the number of S4+ and the increasing of the amount of
S 2
2 −
and S2− in view of the electron transfer after the light treatment.
All the chemical reagents used in this work were of analytical grade and commercially available unless otherwise noted.
The CQDs were synthesized by an electrochemical etching method reported by our group [13].

The CQDs/MoS2 composite was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Firstly, Na2MoO4 (0.625 g) and l-cysteine (1.0 g) were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water to form a uniform solution by stirring, marked as A. Then, 5 mL water-soluble solution of CQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) was added into a 15 mL of the A mixed solution. After that, the mixture was violently stirred for 30 min and then poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the resulting black sediments were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the synthesized solid sample (the weight ratios of the final CQDs/Mo of 1.13%, aliased as CQDs/MoS2) was grinded into powder in a mortar and collected for further measurements. With the same method above, by adding various amounts of CQDs solution into the correspondingly different amounts of the A mixed solution (according to a volume amount of solution of CQDs of 0, 2, 8, 10 mL and a volume amount of A mixed solution of 20, 18, 12, 10 mL, corresponding to the weight ratios calculated by EDS of CQDs/Mo of 0, 0.40%, 2.19% and 2.82%, respectively). The element compositions of the final CQDs/MoS2 composites with different CQDs contents are summarized in Table S1. The little carbon in MoS2 may arise from the l-cysteine in the catalyst synthesis.

The carbon nanotube/MoS2, graphite/MoS2, graphene/MoS2 as electrocatalysts were prepared by similar procedure of CQDs/MoS2. ∼1 mg of graphite (carbon nanotube or graphene) was injected into the A solution instead of the solution of CQDs. The following process was the same as above.

TEM and HRTEM images were obtained with a FEI-Tecnai F20 (200 kV) transmission electron microscope. The TEM samples were prepared by dropping the solution onto a copper grid with polyvinyl formal support film and dried in air. Raman spectra were collected on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (Jobin Yvon, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. XRD was carried out by an X'Pert-ProMPD (Netherland) D/max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). FT-IR spectra of CQDs were obtained with a Varian Spectrum GX spectrometer. XPS was obtained by using a KRATOS Axis Ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray (hν = 1486.6 eV). The powder sample was attached to carbon tape and was set in the XPS chamber. Calibration of binding energy was carried out by setting binding energy of C1s peak to 284.5 eV.

Electrocatalytic measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 920C, CH Instrument, Shanghai) coupled with a rotating disk electrode (RDE) system (ATA-1B, Jiang fen electroanalytical Instrument Co. Ltd., China) in a usual three-electrode electrochemical cell. The rotating disk working electrode made of glassy carbon (geometric area = 0.0707 cm2) loaded with catalyst was used as the working electrode. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum wire were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. To prepare the working electrode, the obtained sample was dispersed in deionized water, reaching a concentration of 2 g/L. After that, the mixture was treated with ultrasonication to generate a homogeneous solution. Next, the dispersion (5 μL) was dropped onto the glassy carbon electrode followed by covering with 5 μL of nafion solution (0.5 wt %). Finally, the treated glassy carbon electrode was dried at room temperature. 0.5 M H2SO4 (pH∼0.3) solution was degassed by bubbling nitrogen for at least 30 min before electrochemical measuring. LSV curves and Tafel plots were measured with a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1. The RDE measurement was conducted with a rotating rate of 1600 rpm. Stability test was measured by CV scanning from 0 to −0.8 V vs. SCE for 1000 laps at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. EIS measurement was measured from 100000 to 0.01 Hz with an AC voltage of 5 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4. In the manuscript, all the potentials were against the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). In 0.5 M H2SO4, E (RHE) = E (SCE) + 0.2412 V + 0.059 pH.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed to analyze the structure and micromorphology of the CQDs/MoS2 composite. The SEM images with different magnifications in Fig. S1 showed that the CQDs/MoS2 held a well-defined three-dimension layered structure composed of numerous sheets. In Fig. 1 a and b, the TEM images with different magnifications further confirmed the typical stacking nanosheet structure of the CQDs/MoS2. It could be seen that the extremely thin nanosheets of CQDs/MoS2 possessed highly exposed edges, and the lateral dimension of sheets was estimated to be about 50–100 nm. This kind of ultrathin sheet was more favorable to generate many exposed edges with more active sites for HER. The direct observation from the high resolution TEM image in Fig. 1c manifested the successful recombination of nanostructured CQDs and MoS2. The lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.63 nm could be assigned to the (002) plane of MoS2 [24]. Meanwhile, the spacing between two fringes of the nanosheet was about 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) planes of CQDs [23]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping images showed the homogeneous distribution of carbon (C), molybdenum (Mo) and sulfur (S) elements across the whole sheets. The EDS pattern in Fig. S2 further confirmed that the CQDs/MoS2 contained carbon, molybdenum and sulfur elements.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was then performed to detect the crystal structure of the prepared catalysts. Fig. 2 a exhibited the XRD patterns of the CQDs, MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2. For the black trace of CQDs, the corresponding XRD profile showed a broad peak centered at approximately 23°, which could be ascribed to the highly amorphous carbon [13]. Both the MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2 samples showed diffraction peaks at about 14°, 33° and 58°, which were assigned to the (002), (100) and (110) facets of MoS2, respectively, suggesting the formation of MoS2 in the composite [25]. Maybe due to the relatively low diffraction intensity and the low amount, the diffraction characteristic peaks for carbon were hardly to be observed in the CQDs/MoS2 sample. The obvious observation for the (002) diffraction of the c axis showed some layered MoS2 nanosheets. The skew symmetric (100) peak in the blue trace of the CQDs/MoS2 indicated the disordered laminar feature, which agreed well with the characterization with SEM and TEM. Raman spectroscopy was used to further confirm the CQDs/MoS2 phase. As shown in Fig. 2b, a D-band at ∼1360 cm−1 and a G-band at ∼1576 cm−1 were observed for the Raman spectrum of CQDs, which were in agreement with previous reports [23]. For the CQDs/MoS2 composite, two characteristic peaks were showed at 374 and 409 cm−1, which were attributed to the
E 1
2 g
and
A 1
2 g
vibrational models of hexagonal MoS2, respectively [10,26,27].

The electrocatalytic activity of the CQDs/MoS2 composite towards HER was investigated on a rotating disk working electrode made of glassy carbon using a typical three-electrode system with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. 3 a revealed the polarization curves for the prepared catalysts. For comparison, we also measured the HER performance of Pt catalyst, which showed extremely high catalytic activity with a very small over potential. It could be seen that both the CQDs and the MoS2 alone showed relatively poor HER activity. In contrast, the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst exhibited good HER catalytic activity with a higher cathodic current density and a positive onset over potential of ∼0.109 V. The little catalytic activity for HER of the physical mixture of MoS2 and CQDs indicated a synergistic effect existed between MoS2 and CQDs rather than a simple physical interaction. In addition, we also prepared another three samples with different initial weight ratios of CQDs/Mo and investigated the HER activities by the overpotential for driving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and the current density at −0.3 V vs RHE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. As shown in Fig. 3b, the catalyst obtained with the weight ratio of CQDs/Mo of 1.13% (as CQDs/MoS2) performed the smallest overpotential of 0.167 V to drive a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The current density at −0.3 V vs RHE for these samples increased in the sequence of CQDs/MoS2-0% (as MoS2) <CQDs/MoS2-2.82% < CQDs/MoS2-0.40% < CQDs/MoS2-2.19% < CQDs/MoS2-1.13% (as CQDs/MoS2). From the results above, we could suggest that the initial weight ratio of precursors had a significant influence on the HER activity of the hybrid catalysts, and the final optimal weight ratio of CQDs/Mo was 1.13% based on a small overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 and a high current density at −0.3 V vs RHE.

In the following study, the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst was irradiated with visible light by a 150 W xenon lamp at room temperature for 30 min (as CQDs/MoS2-L30), which exhibited enhanced HER activity. We also performed the same electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution test on a glassy carbon electrode modified with the CQDs/MoS2 catalysts irradiated for different time. Fig. 4 a showed the polarization curves of the prepared CQDs/MoS2 catalysts, which were irradiated for various time (0, 5, 10, 20 and 30 min). It could be seen that a short time of visible light treatment had little significant improvement for the HER activity of the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst based on the current density and the overpotential. Surprisingly, when we extended the visible light treatment time to 30 min, the CQDs/MoS2 displayed a great enhancement in the catalytic activity towards HER. To our knowledge, the potential value for the current density of 10 mA cm−2 was an important criterion frequently used to estimate the electrochemical catalytic properties. In Fig. 4b, the CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst exhibited a small over potential at 10 mA cm−2 of ∼0.125 V, about 0.042 V more positive than that of CQDs/MoS2 (0.167 V) and the value was better than that of the reported MoS2-based catalysts [6,28,29], suggesting the excellent HER activity of the composite. To better understand the electrode kinetics and interface reactions of the series of CQDs/MoS2 catalysts in HER, we conducted the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement. As shown in Fig. 4c, the CQDs/MoS2-L30 hybrid catalyst revealed the lower faradaic impedance than other CQDs/MoS2 catalysts irradiated for different time. The CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst also displayed a much lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than the pure MoS2 catalyst in Fig. 4d. The small Rct value observed for the CQDs/MoS2-L30 composite implied good electron transfer ability, suggesting the composite was indeed a highly efficient electrocatalyst in the hydrogen evolution reaction.

The Tafel plots yielded from overpotential versus log current density were carried out to further characterize the fluent charge transport. As shown in Fig. 4e, the Tafel slope of CQDs/MoS2-L30 was merely ∼45 mV per decade, which was much lower than that of MoS2 (∼92 mV/decade) and approximately 21 mV/decade lower than that of CQDs/MoS2 (∼66 mV/decade). The small Tafel plot of CQDs/MoS2-L30 close to 40 mV/decade manifested that the electrochemical desorption of Hads and H3O+ to generate H2 was the rate-limiting step, and the hydrogen evolution reduction happened via a Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [5]. The remarkable improvement in charge transport might be mainly attributed to a good electrical contact between the CQDs and MoS2. As both electron acceptors and donors, the CQDs beside electroactive MoS2 nanosheets made the electron transport more efficiently and the interfacial resistance had decreased substantially. Fig. S3 displayed the PL spectra of pure CQDs, MoS2, and the CQD-MoS2 composite. From the remarkable decay of PL intensity in the CQD-MoS2 composite compared with that of pure CQDs, it can be suggested that there should have electron transfer between CQDs and CQD-MoS2 [22]. The long-term potential cycling measurement was performed to evaluate the stability of CQDs/MoS2-30. Fig. 4f exhibited the polarization curves of CQDs/MoS2-30 before and after 1000 cycles. The CQDs/MoS2-30 catalyst showed a negligible loss in catalytic activity with slight current deterioration, indicating an excellent electrochemical durability in 0.5 M H2SO4. Furthermore, the current-time plots at the fixed potential of −0.141 V vs RHE were obtained and displayed in Fig. S4, to further investigate the stability of CQDs/MoS2-30 in HER. It could be observed that the CQDs/MoS2-30 catalyst remained almost invariable after 3000 s, demonstrating the good stability of CQDs/MoS2-30 for HER in 0.5 M H2SO4.

Fig. 5 a showed the FT-IR spectra of CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2-L30, which were used to analyze the surface attached functional groups of the catalysts. The peaks at about 3451 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 were ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of O–H and CO groups, respectively [22]. The band at 1385 cm−1 belonged to CC and the peak at 1125 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C–OH [23]. Interestingly, in the CQDs/MoS2 sample irradiated by 30 min visible light, the absorbance bond for S–O at about 838 cm−1 shifted to 902 cm−1, which might be because of the formation of S–O–C bond [30,31]. Another peak at approximately 621 cm−1 was corresponded to the C–S stretching bond [32]. In addition, the peak at about 475 cm−1 was assigned to Mo–S vibration of MoS2 [33]. With respect to pure CQDs, the CQDs/MoS2 showed a distinctly shift of the C–OH peak (the C–OH peak shifts ∼89 cm−1) in Fig. S5, which might be because of the strong electrostatic interactions between CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs [22].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to identify the chemical state of different elements in the CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2-L30 composites. As shown in Fig. S6, the XPS survey spectrum manifested that the CQDs/MoS2-L30 material contained carbon, oxygen, sulfur and molybdenum elements without other impurities, which was consistent with the EDS result. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of CQDs/MoS2 of C 1s (Fig. 5b), three characteristic peaks located at 284.7, 286.1 and 288.5 eV were assigned to C–C, C–O/C–O–C and OC–O bonding, respectively [34,35]. In Fig. 5c, the high resolution spectrum of Mo 3d could be divided into six peaks, and the bonding energy at approximate 226.0 eV was attributed to S 2s of MoS2. As for the other peaks, two obvious ones observed at around 228.7 and 231.8 eV were characteristic of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo4+. Another two peaks at about 232.3 and 235.3 eV were fitted into the Mo6+ 3d orbit, which might be due to the incomplete decomposition of MoO4 2−. The existence of the peak at about 230.2 eV suggested that Mo6+ was partially reduced to Mo5+ in the sulfide intermediate phases [7,10,36–39]. From Tables S2 and S4, it can be seen that there was no significant difference for the C 1s and Mo 3d XPS spectra of CQDs/MoS2 and those of CQDs/MoS2-L30. In Table S3, the oxygen-containing surface groups of CQDs had increased after light treatment. The increased amount of Mo4+ in CQDs/MoS2-L30 in Table S5 suggested that the light treatment was beneficial to the reduction of high valence state molybdenum to Mo4+. The typical XPS peak for S 2p of CQDs/MoS2 was illustrated in Fig. 5d (the detailed binding energies of the S 2p spectra were showed in Table S6), where the peaks at about 161.5 eV and 162.8 eV could be ascribed to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbits of S2−, respectively [39]. In addition, the peak centered at 164.3 eV manifested the existence of bridging
S 2
2 −
and/or apical S2− ligand [10,43]. As for the high-energy peak at 169.1 eV, it might be a characteristic of the S4+ species in SO3 2−, which still presented at edges of MoS2 layers after washing [10,39–41]. By contrast, the CQDs/MoS2-L30 showed similar shapes with CQDs/MoS2 in the XPS spectra of S 2p peaks but the S4+ site. Then, we got the proportion of each phase of S in the catalysts by calculating the content of the S 2p bonding configurations. As shown in Table S7, about 7.8% of the S4+ species on the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst surface decreased after the visible light treatment. This might be due to the electrons from the large π-conjugated system of CQDs transferred to the S4+ through the O of the oxygen-containing functional groups, thus reducing the S4+ and increasing the amount of
S 2
2 −
and S2−. The more bridging disulfides
S 2
2 −
and apical S2− were highly active edge sites for HER, which could facilitate the adsorption of hydrogen atoms. We believe that the increased amount of unsaturated sulfur atoms should contribute a lot to the enhanced electrocatalytic activity towards HER of the CQDs/MoS2-L30 composite [7,10,39,42,43].

In order to verify it is specific to activate the MoS2 electrocatalyst on CQD's after light treatment, we also prepared MoS2-L30, carbon nanotube/MoS2-L30, graphite/MoS2-L30 and graphene/MoS2-L30 and recorded the polarization curves in 0.5 M H2SO4. In Fig. S7, it could be seen that the CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst displayed the highest electrochemical activity with distinct smallest onset potential and largest current density. The little improvement in HER of other samples may be due to the unsuccessful combination between the MoS2 and the carbon materials. The CQDs with small size and abundant functional groups on the surface facilitated the interaction with MoS2 to form a stabilized composite. After irradiation on the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst, the π-electrons of CQDs were transferred to the S4+, leading to much more
S 2
2 −
and S2−, which greatly improved the electrocatalytic activity for HER.

We further studied the influence of various lights on the HER activity of our CQDs/MoS2 catalysts by linear sweep voltammetry measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. In Fig. S8, the CQDs/MoS2 composite irradiated with UV-light for 30 min performed tiny improvement of HER activity with a little increased current. However, after irradiation with visible light in the same condition, the CQDs/MoS2 catalyst exhibited significant enhancement in HER with a larger current density, especially the more positive onset potential. The results suggested that the visible light illumination had an important positive effect on the HER activity of the CQDs/MoS2 composite in an acid medium. Fig. S9 showed the UV–vis spectra of MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2 samples. It could be seen that the CQDs/MoS2 composite had low absorbability under UV light, and strong absorbability under visible light. The little improvement in HER after applying UV light in CQDs/MoS2 may be due to the low absorption and the photocorrosion in the ultraviolet region [44].

In the followed experiments, we further confirmed this kind of visible-light induced high HER catalytic ability on one electrode. Before the light irradiation, the electrode was a CQDs/MoS2 modified electrode for (1) and (2) in Fig. 6 a. While, after the 30 min irradiation, it would be changed to the CQDs/MoS2-L30 modified electrode for (1′) and (2′) in Fig. 6b. Here, the current density-potential (J-P) characteristics in several on–off cycles under visible-light irradiation were performed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. 6 (1) and (1′) displayed the polarization curves recorded from the cathodes of different cycles under visible light on and off conditions, in which, the HER current was enhanced by adding the visible-light illumination for both CQDs/MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalysts. On the other hand, Fig. 6(2) and (2′) exhibited the overpotentials required to drive 10 mA cm−2 of various cycles under visible light on and off conditions. By contrast, the electrode modified with CQDs/MoS2-L30 showed cathodic current of 10 mA cm−1 at an overpotential of 0.125 V, about 0.042 more positive than that of electrode without irradiation (modified with CQDs/MoS2; overpotential of 0.167 V at 10 mA cm−2). In the further control experiment, under visible-light irradiation, an obvious and stable photocurrent response was also observed for the above electrodes (before and after the light irradiation, shown in Fig. S10), which was different from the visible-light induced high HER catalytic ability.

We presented the design and synthesis of the CQDs/MoS2 composite with various initial weight ratios of CQDs/Mo via a facile hydrothermal method, among which the electrocatalyst with the CQDs/Mo of 1.13% (as CQDs/MoS2) performed the best HER activity. The new-type electrocatalyst irradiated by visible light for 30 min exhibited more efficient electrocatalytic activity towards HER with a small overpotential of ∼0.125 V at 10 mA cm−2, a high current density, a small Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade and good stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. By FT-IR spectra together with XPS spectra, we attributed the great improvement of HER activity of the CQDs/MoS2-L30 catalyst to the charge transfer efficiency, the reducing of S4+ and the increasing of the amount of
S 2
2 −
and S2− after the light treatment. This kind of surface modification strategy makes the electrocatalytic property design of semiconductor catalysts easy to achieve, either experiment operation or component control. Our results will also provide a potential alternative approach to get the high efficient and low-cost electrocatalysts by replacing the components in the system, such as introducing new metal nanoparticles or metallic oxide, and other heteroatom doped carbons.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51132006, 51572179, 21501126, 21471106), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology (ZXG2012028), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.12.088.
